canto_title: The Metamorphosis of the Seventh Weight

scenes.summary: A thief attempts to ward off serpents with his hands, but they entwine themselves around his neck and arms.
Dante laments the fate of Pistoia before witnessing a proud centaur flee in silence.
A raging centaur calls out, and Dante observes a dragon with open wings resting on a beast's back.
Virgil identifies the dragon as Cacus and explains his punishment for theft following the labor of Hercules.
As the dragon passes, three unidentified spirits approach Dante and Virgil.
One spirit speaks to another, and Dante signals Virgil to remain alert during the exchange.
A six-legged serpent strikes one spirit, wrapping its body around the other's limbs and waist.
The two spirits begin to melt together like hot wax, creating a murky, intermediate color.
The other spirits witness the two figures merging into a single, indistinguishable face.
The limbs and torso of the spirits merge into a single, monstrous, and unnatural anatomy.

scenes.scene_name: The Attack of the Small Serpent
scenes.summary: A small, pepper-colored serpent darts forward and pierces one of the merged figures.
The pierced figure reacts with a yawn, while smoke rises and clashes between the wound and the mouth.
Dante calls for silence and compares this transformation to the mythological transformations of Ovid.
The fused creature begins to reshape its anatomy, with the skin of one becoming soft and the other hard.
The arms and legs of the creature lengthen and shorten in a disturbing, asymmetrical manner.
Smoke covers the creature as its skin and hair change color and pattern, while the figures shift faces.
The protruding part of the creature swells, causing its ears to migrate and its nose and lips to thicken.
The creature's head retracts like a snail, and its single tongue splits into a forked one.
The transformed, proud spirit flees through the valley, while the other continues to speak.
Dante reflects on the sight of the seventh weight changing and asks for excuse for his difficult description.
Despite his confusion, Dante recognizes one of the original three spirits who has remained unchanged.
